result:
At dawn, the city forgets to be hurried. Windows soften from black to blue, and the first buses sigh at the curb like patient animals. Someone unlocks a bakery. Someone else waters geraniums on a balcony. The ordinary world begins again, quietly insisting that repetition can be a kind of mercy.

I like these unnoticed thresholds, the moments before names harden around things. A cup is not yet a fuel source, only warmth held between two hands. A street is not yet a route, only a ribbon of possible meetings. Even the mind, before it remembers its tasks, is briefly spacious, a room with all the curtains open.

Perhaps that is why people keep making art, maps, meals, jokes, gardens, and plans. Each is a small argument against the idea that life is merely endured. To draw a line, plant a seed, mend a sleeve, or tell a story is to announce that attention matters. It says the world has not exhausted its invitations.

Later, noise will gather. Phones will flash. Deadlines will lower their weather over desks and doorways. We will become practical, clever, distracted. Still, beneath the day, that early blue remains. It waits in pauses, in the kettle's steam, in the quick smile of a stranger, in the shadow of leaves moving across a wall.

If I had one wish for any afternoon, it would be this: notice one thing without needing it. Let it be enough that it exists, briefly, beside you, asking nothing at all.

Later, noise will gather. Phones will flash. Deadlines will lower their weather over desks and doorways. We will become practical, clever, distracted. Still, beneath the day, that early blue remains.
